sections.content: Overview

Large Language Models have fundamentally transformed natural language processing by introducing a unified architecture capable of handling diverse linguistic tasks without task-specific training. Traditional NLP systems required separate models for sentiment analysis, machine translation, named entity recognition, and question answering—each demanding substantial labeled data collection and feature engineering. The emergence of transformer-based architectures combined with self-supervised learning on massive unlabeled corpora created models that learn universal linguistic representations transferable across virtually any downstream task. This paradigm shift is critical because it addresses a longstanding challenge in machine learning: the sample efficiency problem. By pre-training on trillions of tokens from internet-scale text, LLMs develop rich contextual understanding that captures semantic relationships, grammatical structures, factual knowledge, and reasoning patterns implicitly encoded in language. Without this foundation, systems would require exponentially more labeled examples for each specific task, making practical AI deployment prohibitively expensive. The breakthrough lies in scaling laws—the observation that model performance improves predictably with increases in model size, dataset size, and compute budget, enabling systematic improvement through engineering rather than algorithmic innovation alone.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider how Virat Kohli approaches his batting career across different formats and conditions. Early in his career, Kohli trained extensively in domestic cricket—playing hundreds of matches across formats, building muscle memory for different deliveries, understanding pitch behavior, and developing instinctive responses to various bowling strategies. This accumulated experience (analogous to pre-training on massive text) created a foundational batting philosophy that transfers seamlessly to Test cricket, ODIs, T20s, and even different grounds worldwide. When Kohli faces a new bowling attack or unfamiliar venue, he doesn't need to relearn batting fundamentals—his core techniques, spatial awareness, and decision-making framework apply immediately, requiring only minor tactical adjustments. The universal batting skills developed through years of diverse match experience function exactly like LLM pre-training: broad exposure to varied scenarios creates generalizable knowledge that solves new problems efficiently. Without this foundation, a batsman facing unfamiliar conditions would struggle dramatically, much like an AI model without pre-training struggles with downstream tasks. The insight this reveals is that both expertise and AI systems benefit most from diverse, scaled exposure to foundational patterns before specializing—the breadth of pre-training experience is what enables flexible application.
Core Concepts
Prompt Engineering and In-Context Learning

Prompt engineering is the practice of crafting input text to guide LLMs toward desired outputs, leveraging the model's ability to learn from context within a single forward pass. Unlike traditional machine learning where model parameters are optimized through gradient descent for each new task, LLMs exhibit remarkable few-shot and zero-shot capabilities—performing tasks described only in the prompt without any parameter updates. This capability emerges from the transformer architecture's attention mechanism, which allows the model to recognize patterns in the prompt itself and apply those patterns to generate appropriate completeness. The effectiveness of prompts depends critically on specificity, example formatting, chain-of-thought structuring, and task description clarity. For instance, the same model can perform code generation, mathematical reasoning, translation, summarization, or creative writing depending entirely on how the prompt frames the task. This is powerful because it eliminates the need for task-specific fine-tuning, but it also introduces a new challenge: discovering effective prompts requires experimentation and understanding of how models interpret language. Production systems often employ prompt templates, dynamic few-shot example selection, and retrieval-augmented generation to enhance reliability. The fundamental insight is that for sufficiently large models, the prompt becomes the interface between human intent and model capability, making prompt quality as critical as model quality itself.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: When cricket commentator Harsha Bhogle provides real-time analysis of a match, he might say to viewers: 'If this bowler continues bowling short deliveries on off-stump like he did in the previous over, the batter will likely move inside the crease and play a pull shot to the leg side.' By explicitly describing the scenario (short delivery, off-stump line), previous pattern (previous over behavior), and expected outcome (pull shot to leg), Bhogle structures how viewers think about the match. An LLM receiving a prompt functions identically: the prompt context (like Bhogle's description) sets expectations and examples that guide the model's 'thinking' about the task ahead. If you ask a model 'Summarize this cricket scorecard' with no example, it might respond in any format; but if you provide two examples showing summaries in a specific style, the model adapts to that pattern within the single response generation. Different prompts are like different match situations—the same player (model) behaves differently depending on context (bowling conditions, field placement, match situation). The insight this reveals is that language models are fundamentally contextual machines: they don't have fixed behaviors for tasks, but rather recognize patterns from prompt context and extrapolate those patterns in their output.
Attention Mechanisms and Context Windows

The attention mechanism is the computational core of modern LLMs, enabling the model to selectively focus on relevant parts of input text when making predictions about subsequent tokens. In a transformer architecture, each token attends to all previous tokens through learned weight matrices (Query, Key, Value), computing attention scores that determine how much each token should influence the current prediction. This is crucial because it solves the sequential dependency problem: earlier layers of neural networks cannot meaningfully process long-range dependencies, but attention allows the model to directly correlate words separated by hundreds or thousands of tokens. The context window—the maximum number of tokens a model can process in a single forward pass—is directly limited by attention's quadratic complexity: computing attention between N tokens requires O(N²) operations and O(N²) memory. This creates a fundamental trade-off: larger context windows enable better long-document understanding and coherence but demand exponentially more compute. Modern models employ techniques like rotary positional embeddings, multi-head attention (allowing parallel attention patterns), and flash attention (optimized GPU kernels) to manage this complexity. Production LLMs typically support 4K to 200K token context windows depending on inference hardware and latency requirements. The capability to handle long context is transformational for real-world tasks: processing complete documents, maintaining multi-turn conversation history, and performing complex reasoning steps all depend on sufficient context window.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a cricket fielding captain like Rohit Sharma positioning his eleven players and making real-time decisions during an over. When Rohit decides where to place a fielder for the next delivery, he simultaneously considers: the batter's strengths against this particular bowler (long-range historical dependency), recent deliveries in this over (nearby context), field placements that worked two overs ago (moderate-range dependency), and the overall match situation (entire innings context). Rohit's attention to different information sources varies—he prioritizes the current batter's weakness but also recalls patterns from similar match situations months earlier. This is exactly how the attention mechanism works: the model computes relevance scores (attention weights) for every previous token when predicting the next token, weighting recent tokens, important historical context, and semantically relevant information differently. The context window is like how far back a captain's memory reliably extends: Rohit might remember a batter's tendency from matches two weeks ago (long context), but if matches happened six months ago, details fade (context window limit). Just as a captain managing eleven fielders simultaneous positions requires careful allocation of focus, attention computing correlations between all tokens is expensive, creating the quadratic scaling problem. The insight this reveals is that both cricket captaincy and language understanding operate through selective, weighted attention to past information rather than uniform processing—strong performance requires remembering relevant history and weighted prioritization of influences.
Tokenization and Vocabulary Management

Tokenization is the preprocessing step that converts raw text into discrete token IDs that the model's embedding layer maps to vector representations. Most modern LLMs use byte-pair encoding (BPE) or subword tokenization, splitting words into smaller units—enabling the model to represent uncommon words as combinations of common subword tokens and dramatically reducing vocabulary size. The vocabulary size (typically 50K to 100K tokens) is a critical hyperparameter: smaller vocabularies reduce model parameter count but require longer token sequences to represent text (increasing compute cost), while larger vocabularies increase parameters but reduce sequence length. This trade-off affects training efficiency, inference speed, and the model's ability to represent rare words and multilingual content. For instance, a model with a 50K vocabulary might represent 'uncharacteristically' as six tokens, while a model with larger vocabulary might represent it as two tokens—affecting how efficiently the model learns associations involving that word. The choice of tokenizer also affects model behavior in subtle ways: some tokenizers split URLs poorly, represent numbers token-inefficiently, or create unnecessary boundaries within words. Multilingual models face additional complexity because different languages have varying morphological structure and phonetic characteristics, requiring careful vocabulary design to balance representation efficiency across languages. Many production systems implement language-specific tokenizers within their LLM infrastructure to ensure efficient representation of domain-specific terminology while maintaining compatibility with pre-trained vocabulary.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider how a cricket commentator describes a complex play using different levels of granularity. A simple play might be described as 'Bumrah bowls a yorker for a wicket.' But a complex inning-turning moment might be described with more detail: 'Jasprit Bumrah delivers a precision yorker at 145 kmph targeting the stumps with exceptional movement off the seam, which edges the bat handle and is caught by Pant.' The commentator chooses between atomic descriptions (complete plays as single concepts) and granular descriptions (breaking plays into component parts—pace, movement, contact, outcome). Similarly, tokenization chooses between atomic word tokens (treating each word as indivisible) versus subword tokens (breaking words into meaningful components). If commentary vocabulary includes every possible cricket play ever performed (huge vocabulary), each play is described atomically efficiently. But if vocabulary is limited to basic components (small vocabulary), complex plays require many-part descriptions: 'dot ball on off stump with spin inducing false shot.' The efficiency trade-off is direct: uncommon cricket terms like 'duckworth-lewis' method might require five subword tokens ('duck', 'worth', 'lew', 'is', 'method') in small-vocabulary systems, versus one token in large-vocabulary systems. Different languages create different efficiency: English word structures tokenize efficiently, but agglutinative languages like Turkish (where words have many suffixes) tokenize into many subword tokens using identical vocabulary sizes. The insight this reveals is that representation granularity profoundly affects efficiency and model learning—just as multi-word commentary requires more time to deliver complex concepts, smaller-vocabulary models require longer token sequences to express sophisticated ideas.
Temperature, Top-K, and Sampling Strategy

During inference, LLMs generate the next token by computing a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary, then sampling according to one of several strategies that control the distribution's shape and diversity. Temperature scaling divides logits (pre-softmax scores) by a temperature parameter: temperature < 1.0 makes the distribution sharper (concentrating probability on highest-scoring tokens), while temperature > 1.0 flattens the distribution (increasing randomness). This simple transformation profoundly affects output characteristics—low temperature produces deterministic, conservative text (ideal for fact retrieval tasks), while high temperature produces creative, diverse, sometimes incoherent text (suitable for brainstorming). Top-K sampling restricts the vocabulary to the K most probable tokens before sampling, preventing the model from occasionally picking extremely unlikely tokens that destroy coherence. Similarly, nucleus sampling (top-p) restricts sampling to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds threshold p, achieving adaptive vocabulary restriction that responds to the distribution shape. These techniques are essential for production systems because pure greedy decoding (always selecting highest-probability token) produces repetitive, dull text, while unrestricted sampling sometimes generates nonsensical output. The choice of sampling strategy embodies a fundamental trade-off: deterministic outputs maximize quality and consistency but reduce expressiveness, while stochastic sampling enables creativity but introduces variance. Production LLMs employ different strategies for different use cases—search engines use low temperature to ensure factual correctness, creative writing assistants use higher temperature with top-K constraints to balance coherence and novelty.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Rohit Sharma deciding his batting approach for different match situations. In a Test match where India is 50-0 with three days remaining (need stability), Rohit adopts a conservative approach: he focuses only on the highest-percentage scoring shots (classic drives, straight pushes), restricts aggressive shots, and plays deterministically based on line and length—this is low-temperature sampling. His shot selection becomes predictable because the probability distribution heavily weights established techniques. Conversely, in a T20 match where India needs 18 runs in the final over, Rohit shifts to creative shot-making: he considers audacious reverse sweeps, unconventional ramps, and improvised hitting that he'd normally avoid. He expands his shot vocabulary significantly (higher temperature), but still restricts himself to shots that are physically realistic (top-K sampling)—he won't attempt impossible fielding catches. He doesn't consider dropping the bat and running (nucleus sampling constraint), even though occasionally this might work against lazy fielders. In high-pressure situations where miscommunication is catastrophic (like discussing critical strategy), Rohit becomes maximally deterministic (temperature near zero), speaking factually without creative elaboration. The direct mapping: temperature controls the 'boldness' of shot selection, top-K prevents obviously terrible choices (like swinging at yorkers), and nucleus sampling maintains shots that remain coherent batting strategy. The insight this reveals is that both skilled batters and language models adjust their decision-making conservatism based on context—reliability demands concentrated probability on established techniques, while innovation demands exploring broader possibility space within reasonable constraints.

import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict
from enum import Enum

class SamplingStrategy(Enum):
    GREEDY = "greedy"
    TEMPERATURE = "temperature"
    TOP_K = "top_k"
    NUCLEUS = "nucleus"

@dataclass
class CricketTokenizerVocab:
    """Maps cricket terminology to tokens for efficient representation."""
    vocab_dict: Dict[str, int]
    reverse_vocab: Dict[int
sections.config:
LLMInferenceConfig):
        self.vocab = vocab
        self.config = config
        # Simulated model logits for next token prediction
        self.logits = np.random.randn(vocab.vocab_size) * 2
    
    def apply_temperature_scaling(self
sections.temperature:
float) -> np.ndarray:
        """Apply temperature scaling to logits.
        
        Args:
            logits: Raw model logits (pre-softmax scores)
            temperature: Scaling factor (< 1.0 = sharper, > 1.0 = flatter)
        
        Returns:
            Temperature-scaled logits
        """
        if temperature <= 0:
            raise ValueError("Temperature must be positive")
        return logits / temperature
    
    def compute_probabilities(self

sections.logits:
np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Convert logits to probability distribution via softmax."""
        exp_logits = np.exp(logits - np.max(logits))  # Numerical stability
        return exp_logits / np.sum(exp_logits)
    
    def sample_greedy(self

np.ndarray) -> int:
        """Greedy decoding: always select highest-probability token."""
        return np.argmax(logits)
    
    def sample_temperature_based(self

sections.temperature:
float) -> int:
        """Temperature-scaled sampling with softmax."""
        scaled_logits = self.apply_temperature_scaling(logits, temperature)
        probs = self.compute_probabilities(scaled_logits)
        return np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs)
    
    def sample_top_k(self

sections.k:
int) -> int:
        """Top-K sampling: restrict to K most probable tokens."""
        top_k_indices = np.argsort(logits)[-k:]
        # Zero out non-top-K logits
        filtered_logits = np.full_like(logits, -np.inf)
        filtered_logits[top_k_indices] = logits[top_k_indices]
        probs = self.compute_probabilities(filtered_logits)
        return np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs)
    
    def sample_nucleus(self

sections.p:
float) -> int:
        """Nucleus (top-p) sampling: restrict to smallest set with cumulative prob >= p."""
        probs = self.compute_probabilities(logits)
        sorted_indices = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]  # Descending order
        cumsum_probs = np.cumsum(probs[sorted_indices])
        
        # Find cutoff where cumulative probability exceeds p
        nucleus_indices = sorted_indices[cumsum_probs <= p]
        
        # Zero out non-nucleus tokens
        filtered_logits = np.full_like(logits, -np.inf)
        filtered_logits[nucleus_indices] = logits[nucleus_indices]
        filtered_probs = self.compute_probabilities(filtered_logits)
        
        return np.random.choice(len(filtered_probs), p=filtered_probs)
    
    def generate_cricket_narrative(self
sections.prompt_tokens:
List[str]) -> str:
        """Generate cricket commentary using configured sampling strategy."""
        narrative = prompt_tokens.copy()
        
        for step in range(self.config.max_new_tokens):
            # Simulate attention over context window
            context_window_start = max(0, len(narrative) - self.config.context_window)
            context_tokens = narrative[context_window_start:]
            
            # In real LLM, this would be forward pass through transformer
            # For simulation, we perturb logits based on last token
            if context_tokens:
                last_token = context_tokens[-1]
                # Create context-dependent logits (cricket terms should be more probable)
                logits = self.logits.copy()
                for cricket_term in ['wicket', 'boundary', 'maiden', 'yorker']:
                    if cricket_term in self.vocab.vocab_dict:
                        logits[self.vocab.vocab_dict[cricket_term]] += 2.0
            else:
                logits = self.logits.copy()
            
            # Select next token based on sampling strategy
            if self.config.sampling_strategy == SamplingStrategy.GREEDY:
                next_token_idx = self.sample_greedy(logits)
            elif self.config.sampling_strategy == SamplingStrategy.TEMPERATURE:
                next_token_idx = self.sample_temperature_based(logits, self.config.temperature)
            elif self.config.sampling_strategy == SamplingStrategy.TOP_K:
                next_token_idx = self.sample_top_k(logits, self.config.top_k)
            elif self.config.sampling_strategy == SamplingStrategy.NUCLEUS:
                next_token_idx = self.sample_nucleus(logits, self.config.top_p)
            
            next_token = self.vocab.reverse_vocab.get(next_token_idx, f'<unk_{next_token_idx}>')
            narrative.append(next_token)
        
        return ' '.join(narrative)

# Usage demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create cricket-specialized vocabulary
    vocab = CricketTokenizerVocab.create_cricket_vocab()
    
    # Test different sampling strategies
    prompt = ['Rohit', 'Sharma', 'plays']
    
    strategies = [
        (SamplingStrategy.GREEDY, LLMInferenceConfig(sampling_strategy=SamplingStrategy.GREEDY, max_new_tokens=10)),
        (SamplingStrategy.TEMPERATURE, LLMInferenceConfig(temperature=0.7, sampling_strategy=SamplingStrategy.TEMPERATURE, max_new_tokens=10)),
        (SamplingStrategy.TOP_K, LLMInferenceConfig(top_k=5, sampling_strategy=SamplingStrategy.TOP_K, max_new_tokens=10)),
        (SamplingStrategy.NUCLEUS, LLMInferenceConfig(top_p=0.9, sampling_strategy=SamplingStrategy.NUCLEUS, max_new_tokens=10)),
    ]
    
    print("Cricket LLM Inference Demonstration")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    for strategy_name, config in strategies:
        inference = CricketLLMInference(vocab, config)
        generated = inference.generate_cricket_narrative(prompt)
        print(f"\nStrategy: {strategy_name.value}")
        print(f"Config: temp={config.temperature}, top_k={config.top_k}, top_p={config.top_p}")
        print(f"Generated: {generated}")
    
    # Demonstrate context window and attention
    print("\n" + "=" * 50)
    print("Context Window Demonstration")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    long_sequence = ['Bumrah'] * 100 + ['bowls'] + ['a'] + ['yorker']
    config_short_context = LLMInferenceConfig(context_window=10)
    config_long_context = LLMInferenceConfig(context_window=100)
    
    inference_short = CricketLLMInference(vocab, config_short_context)
    inference_long = CricketLLMInference(vocab, config_long_context)
    
    print(f"\nWith context_window=10: Attends to last 10 tokens of 102-token sequence")
    print(f"With context_window=100: Attends to last 100 tokens of 102-token sequence")
    print(f"Longer context enables better understanding of earlier 'Bumrah' repetitions")

sections.content: The code above demonstrates all four core concepts working together in a unified inference pipeline. First, the `CricketTokenizerVocab` class implements tokenization by mapping cricket terminology to integer token IDs, creating a domain-specialized vocabulary that improves efficiency for cricket-related text—this is tokenization in action. Second, the `LLMInferenceConfig` dataclass encapsulates sampling parameters: temperature, top_k, and top_p, which control probability distribution shape during generation. Third, the `CricketLLMInference` class implements the attention-based inference loop, respecting the `context_window` parameter to simulate how many previous tokens influence the next prediction (lines managing `context_window_start` show attention's sequential dependency). Fourth, the sampling methods demonstrate how different strategies produce different behaviors: greedy selection is deterministic (always argmax), temperature scaling flattens or sharpens distributions, top_K restricts vocabulary, and nucleus sampling adapts vocabulary based on probability mass. The generation loop produces narrative sequences respecting the context window constraint, with vocabulary probabilities modified when cricket-specific terms appear (simulating how real transformer attention weights increase relevant token probabilities). When you call `generate_cricket_narrative`, the system generates text token-by-token, computing probabilities for the next token given context, then sampling according to the configured strategy. Low-temperature generation produces repetitive but coherent cricket commentary, while high-temperature generation includes more diverse (sometimes nonsensical) word choices. The `context_window` parameter determines how much earlier context influences current predictions—demonstrated in the final section where 100 previous 'Bumrah' tokens influence understanding better when context_window=100 versus context_window=10.
How It Works Under the Hood

The internal mechanics of LLM inference involve cascading tensor operations optimized for GPUs and specialized hardware accelerators. When text enters the system, the tokenizer converts strings to token IDs in O(n) linear time, where n is text length. These IDs index into the embedding table (a parameter matrix of size vocab_size × embedding_dimension), producing dense vectors for each token. The transformer decoder then processes these vectors through multiple layers, each containing multi-headed attention followed by feed-forward networks. Multi-headed attention (typically 8-96 heads) computes query-key-value operations: each head independently computes attention weights over all previous tokens using learned projection matrices, then aggregates information from high-attention tokens. This is where the quadratic complexity emerges: for a sequence of length L, computing L×L attention matrices requires O(L²) floating-point operations per attention head, aggregated across all layers and heads. Modern implementations use optimized CUDA kernels (like FlashAttention) that fuse multiple operations and reduce memory bandwidth requirements, achieving 2-4x speedups compared to naive implementations. The feed-forward networks consist of two learned linear transformations with a nonlinearity (typically GELU), processing each position independently. After all transformer layers, the output embeddings are projected to vocabulary logits (dimension vocab_size) via a linear layer, then softmax produces token probabilities. Sampling according to chosen strategy produces the next token, which is appended to the sequence, and the process repeats—each new token requires another full forward pass through all layers. For production inference at scale, systems implement batching (processing multiple sequences simultaneously), key-value caching (storing intermediate results to avoid recomputation), and dynamic batching (grouping sequences with similar lengths for efficiency). A typical inference serving system might process 100-1000 requests concurrently on a single GPU cluster, managing memory carefully because storing activations for all layers and attention heads can consume gigabytes per batch. The latency breakdown typically shows: tokenization (< 1ms), embedding lookup (<1ms), attention computation (50-80% of latency), feed-forward computation (15-40% of latency), logits softmax and sampling (<5ms), and batching overhead—resulting in typical latencies of 50-200ms per output token depending on model size and hardware.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a massive cricket training facility analyzing live match data to predict Virat Kohli's next shot. The process mirrors LLM inference mechanics. First, a video technician (tokenizer) converts continuous video footage into discrete frame segments, each labeled with shot ID: frame-52 shows Kohli's stance, frame-53 shows bat pickup, frame-54 shows ball approaching. These discrete frames (tokens) are stored in a reference library with associated video embeddings (tokenizer → embedding table). Next, a team of cricket analysts (transformer layers) simultaneously examine these frames: one analyst specializes in detecting pull-shot patterns (attention head 1), another specializes in defensive technique (attention head 2), another in fielding positioning context (attention head 3). Each analyst independently computes relevance scores (attention weights) for how much each previous frame influences the current prediction—for instance, head-1 gives high weight to frame-52 (stance shows pull-shot setup) but lower weight to frame-48 (10 frames back, less relevant). The analysts then aggregate their observations: head-1 says 'pull-shot probability 0.7', head-2 says 'defensive shot probability 0.2', head-3 says 'drive probability 0.1'. These predictions are refined through multiple analysis rounds (transformer layers), with each round incorporating the previous round's insights (residual connections). The final prediction is a probability distribution over all possible shots (softmax logits): ['pull-shot': 0.65, 'drive': 0.20, 'defend': 0.10, 'leave': 0.05]. The coaching staff then selects the next shot according to strategy: greedy selection always picks pull-shot, temperature scaling adjusts confidence in this prediction, and top-K sampling might exclude 'leave' as too unlikely. The entire process repeats for the next ball, with analysts attending to all previous balls in the over (context window). If the over is 17 balls long (exceeding typical attention memory), analysts might not remember details from the 1st ball with perfect clarity (attention falloff beyond context window). The insight this reveals is that both real-time cricket analysis and language model inference use parallel, multi-headed evaluation (multiple specialists) with selective attention to relevant history, bounded by practical memory and computation constraints that prevent perfect recall of all context.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from typing import Tuple, Optional
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TransformerConfig:
    """Configuration for transformer architecture."""
    vocab_size: int = 500
    embedding_dim: int = 64
    num_heads: int = 4
    num_layers: int = 2
    ff_hidden_dim: int = 256
    context_window: int = 32
    dropout: float = 0.1

class CricketAttentionHead(nn.Module):
    """Single attention head for cricket match analysis."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.context_window:
int):
        super().__init__()
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.context_window = context_window
        self.head_dim = embedding_dim
        
        # Query, Key, Value projections for this head
        self.query = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, self.head_dim)
        self.key = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, self.head_dim)
        self.value = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, self.head_dim)
        
        # Scaling factor for numerical stability
        self.scale = np.sqrt(self.head_dim)
    
    def forward(self
sections.context_mask:
Optional[torch.Tensor] = None) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Compute attention for one head.
        
        Args:
            x: Input tensor of shape [batch, seq_len, embedding_dim]
            context_mask: Optional mask to enforce context window. Shape [batch, seq_len, seq_len]
        
        Returns:
            Attention output of shape [batch, seq_len, head_dim]
        """
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape
        
        # Project to Q, K, V
        Q = self.query(x)  # [batch, seq_len, head_dim]
        K = self.key(x)    # [batch, seq_len, head_dim]
        V = self.value(x)  # [batch, seq_len, head_dim]
        
        # Compute attention scores: Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k)
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / self.scale  # [batch, seq_len, seq_len]
        
        # Apply context window mask: tokens can only attend to recent history
        if context_mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(context_mask, float('-inf'))
        
        # Apply softmax to get attention weights
        attn_weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)  # [batch, seq_len, seq_len]
        attn_weights = torch.nan_to_num(attn_weights)  # Handle -inf from masking
        
        # Weight values by attention
        output = torch.matmul(attn_weights, V)  # [batch, seq_len, head_dim]
        
        return output, attn_weights

class CricketMultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Multi-head attention simulating multiple cricket analysts."""
    
    def __init__(self

sections.context_window:
int):
        super().__init__()
        assert embedding_dim % num_heads == 0
        
        self.embedding_dim = embedding_dim
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embedding_dim // num_heads
        self.context_window = context_window
        
        # Multiple attention heads (multiple cricket analysts)
        self.heads = nn.ModuleList([
            CricketAttentionHead(embedding_dim, context_window) 
            for _ in range(num_heads)
        ])
        
        # Output projection combining all heads
        self.output_proj = nn.Linear(embedding_dim, embedding_dim)
    
    def create_context_mask(self
sections.device:
torch.device) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Create mask to enforce context window constraint.
        
        Positions can attend to all previous positions within context_window,
        but cannot attend to positions beyond the context window.
        """
        # Causal mask: no attending to future positions
        causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, device=device), diagonal=1)
        
        # Context window mask: no attending beyond context window distance
        context_mask = torch.zeros(seq_len, seq_len, device=device)
        for i in range(seq_len):
            # Position i can attend to positions [max(0, i-context_window), i]
            start = max(0, i - self.context_window)
            context_mask[i, :start] = 1
        
        # Combine masks
        combined_mask = (causal_mask + context_mask) > 0
        return combined_mask
    
    def forward(self
sections.x:
torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        """Apply multi-head attention.
        
        Args:
            x: Input tensor [batch, seq_len, embedding_dim]
        
        Returns:
            output: Attention output [batch, seq_len, embedding_dim]
            attention_weights: Attention weights for visualization [num_heads, batch, seq_len, seq_len]
        """
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape
        device = x.device
        
        # Create attention mask respecting context window and causality
        mask = self.create_context_mask(seq_len, device)
        mask = mask.unsqueeze(0)  # Add batch dimension
        
        # Apply each attention head
        head_outputs = []
        attention_maps = []
        
        for head in self.heads:
            head_output, attn_weights = head(x, mask)
            head_outputs.append(head_output)
            attention_maps.append(attn_weights)
        
        # Concatenate all head outputs
        combined = torch.cat(head_outputs, dim=-1)  # [batch, seq_len, embedding_dim]
        
        # Project combined output
        output = self.output_proj(combined)
        
        # Stack attention weights for all heads
        attention_weights = torch.stack(attention_maps, dim=0)  # [num_heads, batch, seq_len, seq_len]
        
        return output, attention_weights

class CricketTransformerLayer(nn.Module):
    """Single transformer layer combining attention and feed-forward."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.config:
TransformerConfig):
        super().__init__()
        
        # Attention sublayer
        self.attention = CricketMultiHeadAttention(
            embedding_dim=config.embedding_dim,
            num_heads=config.num_heads,
            context_window=config.context_window
        )
        
        # Feed-forward sublayer (cricket coaches refining predictions)
        self.feed_forward = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(config.embedding_dim, config.ff_hidden_dim),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(config.ff_hidden_dim, config.embedding_dim)
        )
        
        # Layer normalization and residual connections
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(config.embedding_dim)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(config.embedding_dim)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(config.dropout)
    
    def forward(self

sections.x:
torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        """Process through attention and feed-forward with residuals."""
        # Attention sublayer with residual
        attn_output, attention_weights = self.attention(x)
        x = x + self.dropout(attn_output)
        x = self.norm1(x)
        
        # Feed-forward sublayer with residual
        ff_output = self.feed_forward(x)
        x = x + self.dropout(ff_output)
        x = self.norm2(x)
        
        return x, attention_weights

class CricketLLMDecoder(nn.Module):
    """Complete decoder-only LLM for cricket analysis."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.config:
TransformerConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.config = config
        
        # Embedding layer (token ID to vector)
        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(config.vocab_size, config.embedding_dim)
        
        # Positional embeddings (rotary position embeddings for context awareness)
        self.position_embedding = nn.Embedding(config.context_window, config.embedding_dim)
        
        # Stack of transformer layers
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList([
            CricketTransformerLayer(config) for _ in range(config.num_layers)
        ])
        
        # Output layer (embedding to vocabulary logits)
        self.output_projection = nn.Linear(config.embedding_dim, config.vocab_size)
        
        # Layer norm before output
        self.final_norm = nn.LayerNorm(config.embedding_dim)
    
    def forward(self
sections.token_ids:
torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, list]:
        """Forward pass computing logits and attention weights.
        
        Args:
            token_ids: Tensor of shape [batch, seq_len] with token IDs
        
        Returns:
            logits: Tensor of shape [batch, seq_len, vocab_size]
            all_attention_weights: List of attention weight tensors from each layer
        """
        batch_size, seq_len = token_ids.shape
        
        # Embed tokens
        x = self.token_embedding(token_ids)
        
        # Add position embeddings (clamped to context window)
        positions = torch.arange(seq_len, device=token_ids.device)
        positions = torch.clamp(positions, max=self.config.context_window - 1)
        pos_embed = self.position_embedding(positions)
        x = x + pos_embed
        
        # Process through transformer layers
        all_attention_weights = []
        
        for layer in self.layers:
            x, attn_weights = layer(x)
            all_attention_weights.append(attn_weights)
        
        # Apply final normalization
        x = self.final_norm(x)
        
        # Project to vocabulary logits
        logits = self.output_projection(x)  # [batch, seq_len, vocab_size]
        
        return logits, all_attention_weights
    
    def inference_step(self

sections.temperature:
float = 1.0) -> int:
        """Single inference step: compute next token distribution.
        
        Args:
            token_ids: Current sequence of shape [batch, seq_len]
            temperature: Sampling temperature
        
        Returns:
            next_token_id: Sampled next token ID
        """
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits, _ = self.forward(token_ids)
            
            # Get logits for final position (next token prediction)
            next_logits = logits[:, -1, :]  # [batch, vocab_size]
            
            # Apply temperature scaling
            next_logits = next_logits / temperature
            
            # Softmax to get probabilities
            probabilities = torch.softmax(next_logits, dim=-1)
            
            # Sample next token (for batch size 1)
            next_token = torch.multinomial(probabilities[0], num_samples=1)
            
            return next_token.item()

# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create model configuration
    config = TransformerConfig(
        vocab_size=200,
        embedding_dim=64,
        num_heads=4,
        num_layers=2,
        ff_hidden_dim=256,
        context_window=16
    )
    
    # Initialize LLM
    model = CricketLLMDecoder(config)
    
    # Create sample cricket commentary token sequence
    # (In practice, these would be real token IDs from tokenizer)
    batch_size = 2
    seq_len = 8
    
    sample_tokens = torch.randint(0, config.vocab_size, (batch_size, seq_len))
    
    print("Cricket LLM Architecture Demonstration")
    print("=" * 50)
    print(f"Config: {config}")
    print(f"\nInput token shape: {sample_tokens.shape}")
    
    # Forward pass
    logits, attention_weights = model(sample_tokens)
    
    print(f"Output logits shape: {logits.shape}")
    print(f"Number of transformer layers: {len(attention_weights)}")
    print(f"Attention weights per layer shape: {attention_weights[0].shape}")
    print(f"  (num_heads, batch, seq_len, seq_len)")
    
    # Analyze context window effect
    print("\nContext Window Analysis:")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    # Check which positions each token can attend to
    seq_pos = 15  # Position in sequence
    context_window = config.context_window
    
    can_attend_from = max(0, seq_pos - context_window)
    can_attend_to = seq_pos + 1
    
    print(f"Position {seq_pos}:")
    print(f"  - Can attend to positions [{can_attend_from}, {can_attend_to})")
    print(f"  - Context window size: {context_window}")
    print(f"  - Positions beyond {can_attend_from} are masked out")
    
    # Inference: generate next token
    print("\nInference Step:")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    temperatures = [0.3, 1.0, 1.5]
    
    for temp in temperatures:
        next_token = model.inference_step(sample_tokens, temperature=temp)
        print(f"Temperature {temp}: Next token ID = {next_token}")
    
    # Demonstrate attention flow
    print("\nAttention Head Analysis:")
    print("=" * 50)
    
    layer_idx = 0
    head_idx = 0
    batch_idx = 0
    
    attn_map = attention_weights[layer_idx][head_idx, batch_idx]
    print(f"Layer {layer_idx}, Head {head_idx} attention weights:")
    print(f"  Shape: {attn_map.shape}")
    print(f"  Each row shows attention weights FROM that position")
    print(f"  Row i shows how position i attends to all previous positions")
    print(f"\nAttention weights (position 7 attends to earlier positions):")
    print(f"  {attn_map[-1]}")

sections.content: Pro Tip: In production systems, implement key-value caching during inference to avoid recomputing attention for previous tokens. When generating the Nth token, cache the key and value projections from the first N-1 tokens, so each new forward pass only computes attention for the new token against cached keys/values. This reduces computation from O(N²) per-token to O(N) per-token, enabling inference speed improvements of 5-10x on long sequences. For a model generating 100 tokens, this difference is the gap between multi-second latency and sub-100ms latency.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Several established patterns have emerged from deploying LLMs at scale in production environments. The first is prompt engineering for reliability: rather than expecting models to perform tasks from minimal instruction, successful applications use detailed, example-rich prompts with explicit reasoning steps. Chain-of-thought prompting, where the model is prompted to 'think through' problems step-by-step before providing final answers, dramatically improves performance on reasoning tasks—sometimes by 20-40% accuracy. This works because intermediate reasoning steps serve as constraints that guide the probability distribution toward coherent logic rather than plausible-sounding but incorrect answers. The second pattern is retrieval-augmented generation (RAG): instead of relying on LLM knowledge learned during pre-training (which is static and can be outdated or hallucinatory), systems retrieve relevant documents from a knowledge base, include those documents in the prompt, and have the model generate answers grounded in retrieved facts. This approach is essential for production systems because it separates model capability (language understanding and generation) from knowledge currency (maintaining up-to-date information), allowing systems to be correct even when trained on older data. The third pattern is prompt versioning and systematic evaluation: successful teams treat prompts as code, version-controlling them, running A/B tests across multiple user cohorts, and measuring metrics like output quality, latency, and token efficiency. This statistical rigor prevents the trap of assuming that prompts that work well on individual examples generalize across diverse user scenarios. These patterns address fundamental limitations of LLMs: uncertainty about what they know, inability to access new information automatically, and the tendency to fail silently with confident-sounding but incorrect answers—all of which were unacceptable in production systems before these practices emerged.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider how a world-class cricket team prepares and executes under pressure. The team uses three complementary strategies that mirror LLM best practices. First, strategic briefing before innings mirrors chain-of-thought prompting: rather than expecting batters to improvise, the captain briefs batters explicitly on strategies—'Against Bumrah, focus on leaving short balls outside off-stump, look to drive full deliveries on driving length, and expect bouncers in the first 3 deliveries.' This detailed pre-planning, although it requires more communication time, makes execution more reliable by constraining decisions to strategies that work. A batter trying to improvise every delivery faces 100x more possible decisions, while briefed batters converge toward high-probability winning strategies. Second, information gathering during the match mirrors retrieval-augmented generation: rather than relying only on pre-game analysis (pre-training knowledge), the captain continuously updates strategy based on live information—'The pitch is seaming more than expected, so I'll deploy additional bowlers with seaming attack.' The fielding captain doesn't assume static knowledge; instead, the captain retrieves live match context, interprets it, and regenerates strategy accordingly. This prevents the problem of outdated pre-game knowledge (like assuming flat pitch when it's actually seaming). Third, systematic preparation mirrors prompt versioning: elite teams don't wing strategic decisions; they analyze videos of upcoming opponents, test different batting orders in practice sessions, measure performance metrics (average runs per over, strike rotation effectiveness), and iterate based on evidence. If one opening partnership performs poorly against certain bowlers, they test alternative partnerships statistically before major matches. The insight this reveals is that both elite cricket teams and reliable LLM systems succeed by constraining decision spaces with detailed planning, grounding decisions in current information rather than relying on static knowledge, and systematically validating strategies through measurement rather than intuition.
